You've Got to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - JSX Basics - (4)
You've probably heard people throw around the term "JSX" — but what actually is it? It kind of sounds like one of those things you absolutely have to learn if you want to write React.

Before you write any React, you'll need to understand JSX. And honestly, we've been writing JSX all along in the earlier posts — we just never stopped to look at it properly. So let's get to know what JSX actually is.
Like I just said, we've already written JSX before — maybe you just didn't notice. So... when exactly did we write JSX earlier? Let me pull up one of the previous examples.
See the Pen (6) Hello React - 2 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

JSX isn't HTML, it isn't a string, and it definitely isn't CSS (well, obviously). It's an extension to JavaScript that React introduced. The full name is JavaScript & XML, and it's basically an "HTML in JavaScript" syntax extension (I'll just call it JSX from here on).
So what does the basic JSX syntax look like? It's written like this:

If this is your first time seeing JSX, it might feel a bit like magic. Why magic? Well, normally when you write HTML inside JavaScript you have to wrap it in single quotes, double quotes, or backticks — but with JSX you don't.
And when you try to output the variable
, you'll notice JSX automatically converts into a React Element.
See the Pen (17) React JSX - 1 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
So what does that mean? Back in the second post, "You've Got to Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - Hello React - (2)", we talked about the
See the Pen (18) React JSX - 2 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
I'm pretty sure that when you're actually building something, you don't want to write
every single time you add a new element.
children: 'lorem lorem lorem lorem lorem lorem',
See the Pen (20) React JSX - 4 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
By comparison, using JSX directly is way nicer and easier to learn — it feels almost exactly like writing plain HTML.
See the Pen (20) React JSX - 4 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

Let's keep going with JSX. JSX can also embed an expression.
If you're not sure what an expression is, you can read my earlier post:
JavaScript Core Concepts (11) - Operators, Types and Grammar - Statements and Expressions

Using it is really simple — you just use curly braces (some folks call them braces; I'll just call them curly braces) inside JSX and put a variable in there. As long as it's an expression, it'll work fine.
See the Pen (27) React JSX - Key - 6 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
Since it supports expressions, we can also write functions that return a result.
See the Pen (28) React JSX - Expression - 1 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
You can even write some weird code in there — as long as it's an expression, it all goes.
See the Pen (29) React JSX - Expression - 2 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
itself is also an expression — it just doesn't return anything by default.)
Now, because JSX only accepts expressions, you can't write an
See the Pen (30) React JSX - Expression - 3 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
But we can write a ternary operator inside JSX.
See the Pen (31) React JSX - Expression - 4 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
And this JSX trait is exactly the same as the
block when you're writing a Vue SFC (Single File Component).
JSX also has some special tricks — for instance, it can automatically spread out the contents of an array.
See the Pen (32) React JSX - Expression - 5 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
You can write comments in JSX too — just note that the comment has to go inside curly braces, and don't put the comment on the same line, like this:

const example =

Hello { // this is a comment }

;
See the Pen (33) React JSX - Auto Spread by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
That style of writing ends up commenting out everything after it and throws an error. The correct way is like this:

const example =

Hello { /* this is a comment */ }

;
See the Pen (34) React JSX - Comment - 1 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
So the comments JSX supports are the asterisk-style ones.

When you're actually building stuff, you'll definitely need to add some styling to the page, like HTML Style and HTML Class. But both of these have some details to watch out for in JSX. For example, with HTML Style, note that JSX uses camelCase (meaning whenever you'd hit a
dash, you turn the next letter uppercase instead).
See the Pen (35) React JSX - Comment - 2 by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

You've probably heard people throw around the term "JSX" — but what actually is it? It kind of sounds like one of those things you absolutely have to learn if you want to write React. In this post we'll get to know what JSX really is, walk through its basic syntax, and cover keys, expressions, array spreading, comments, and how styles and classes work differently from plain HTML.
